title: Pensioner — Pension + FD / Savings Interest
description: Retired individual with pension income (treated as salary) and interest from fixed deposits, savings accounts, or post office schemes. Often eligible for ITR-1. Senior citizens (≥60) get enhanced basic exemption and 80TTB deduction (up to ₹50,000 on interest income). Super seniors (≥80) cannot file ITR-1 — must use ITR-2.

prevalence.description: Large and growing segment as India's population ages. Many first-time filers in this category due to TDS on FDs.

typical_evidence.notes: Form 16 from pension-disbursing bank (e.g. SBI, PNB for government pensioners). Not always issued; use 26AS and pension passbook instead.
Form 16A from banks for TDS on FD interest. Required if TDS was deducted (FD interest > ₹50,000 for senior citizens).
26AS is the authoritative source for all TDS credits — pension TDS and FD TDS.
AIS to verify full interest income from all banks and post office. Senior citizens often have FDs spread across multiple banks.

applicable_decisions.notes: Pension is treated as salary for ITR purposes. If age ≥ 80 (super senior), ITR-1 is not available — ITR-2 is required.
New Regime is default. However, senior citizens with significant medical expenses (80D) or Section 80TTB interest deduction of ₹50,000 may benefit from Old Regime. Must compute both.
Pension TDS + FD TDS. Banks deduct TDS on FD at 10% if PAN is furnished, 20% if not. Senior citizen threshold for FD TDS is ₹50,000.
Refund is common — many pensioners with income below taxable limit still have TDS deducted on FDs.

common_validations.notes: Pension TDS from bank may not match 26AS — especially for government pensions where quarterly TDS filing by disbursing bank may be delayed.
FD interest from multiple banks is commonly missed. AIS now shows interest from all SFT-reporting banks. Senior citizens often have FDs in 3-4 banks.
Refund very common — TDS on FDs often exceeds actual tax liability for seniors with income near basic exemption.

intake_questions.question: What is your age as of 31 March 2025?
intake_questions.purpose: Determines age category: General (< 60), Senior Citizen (60-79), Super Senior (≥ 80). Super seniors cannot use ITR-1.

intake_questions.question: Do you receive a government / central / state pension?
intake_questions.purpose: Government pensions are disbursed through banks. Private pensions from annuity plans are also treated as salary but sourcing of Form 16 differs.

intake_questions.question: How many banks do you have Fixed Deposits with?
intake_questions.purpose: FD interest from all banks must be declared. Helps estimate AIS coverage requirement.

intake_questions.question: Have you submitted Form 15H (declaration for no TDS) to your banks?
intake_questions.purpose: If Form 15H was submitted and total income is still below basic exemption, no TDS should have been deducted. If TDS was deducted despite Form 15H, that's an error.

edge_cases.consequence: Super seniors cannot use ITR-1. Must use ITR-2. They also cannot pay advance tax and must pay self-assessment tax only.

edge_cases.condition: Post office MIS / NSC / PPF maturity received
edge_cases.consequence: Post office scheme maturity may be taxable (NSC interest) or exempt (PPF, Sukanya Samridhi). Must categorise correctly. NSC interest appears in AIS.

notes: Section 80TTB allows senior citizens (≥60) to deduct up to ₹50,000 of interest income from savings accounts AND FDs. This is more generous than 80TTA (only savings interest, max ₹10,000 for non-seniors) and is only available in the Old Regime.
